title: If Berkeley were honest #berkeley #college #collegeadmissions
body: Televised college football games include brief commercials from the competing schools extolling their virtues. A recent UCB video, after images of the lovely campus and bright eyed students, ended with the declaration that the university was "of the people, by the people, and for the people" of California. That reminded me that the school's spirit and purpose have always been to educate, stimulate and encourage young people who reflect the remarkable diversity of the state. That fundamental tenet separates UCB from otherwise excellent private schools. It is truly remarkable public university.

title: If Berkeley were honest #berkeley #college #collegeadmissions
body:
Berkeley student here. Stanford does not live rent free in my head, and I'm not a CS student! 😂

Berkeley has a great program for Rhetoric and Film & Media Studies. The former is rarely offered at the same depth anywhere else in the country. The latter, while significantly less focused on production than UCLA and USC to the south, is probably one of the best media theory educations you can get anywhere.

title: 10 THINGS NO ONE TELLS YOU ABOUT UC BERKELEY
body:
Thoughts about these as someone who also went to Berkeley:
CONS:
1. This is highly dependent on the class and your motivation. If you're in a huge freshman math class or something, sure there's going to be a lot of people at office hours, but there's plenty of extra help and resources outside of the prof's office hours. It's not like you're really going to want a letter of rec from a generic class like this anyway. In upper division classes, it was very common for my professors to know me, as barely anyone goes to office hours. It's profs like this in more specialized classes in your major that you enjoy that you would want to write a letter of rec or make connections with anyway.
2. This likely very dependent on your major. I'm in engineering and there was lots of research opportunities. You could easily talk to professors about their research and ask to join their lab. Of course, it's not guaranteed your first choice will say yes, since some labs are too advanced for undergrads or are just really full but there are definitely enough options to find something no problem. I'm guessing premed is harder, I just wanted people to know it's not necessarily the same for everyone.
3. Agreed
4. True, it's definitely not the safest, but there's free night safety shuttles so you certainly don't have to uber home. I went 5 years without encountering any crime personally, so it's definitely not just inevitable.
4.5. Very true, it's a struggle for everyone
5. There are lots of business and CS clubs like this, but outside of that it's much more relaxed. If you don't want to surround yourself with these type of people, it's very easy to stay away from them. If you're looking for clubs for the actual sake of meeting new people and having fun, you'll find a much more wholesome chill atmosphere than if you're just looking for clubs to get some kind of networking advantage. And simply by the nature of meeting people, I'm sure you'll make some meaningful and helpful connections.

I agree with most of the pros. I love that people don't really care what you look like. And that doesn't mean everyone is just lazy, but rather they're much more willing to be different rather than basic. While these types of people surely exist everywhere, I feel like there's generally less fake, self absorbed people than other places, especially compared to LA. It might feel competitive if you're premed or business (which is probably true everywhere), but this girl is both and even she said it's not that bad. I personally never experienced people being competitive, rather the opposite, everyone felt like they were struggling and we could kind of bond over that and go through it together. Although I disagree that campus is easy to get around. It's quite hilly and that can definitely make it a struggle and rule out biking/skating for many people. But it is nicely located near tons of restaurants, bars, etc so you definitely won't need a car which is nice. Plus I just love the city along with Oakland and SF, there's plenty to do and easy enough to get around.
author: @TacosAndBoba

title: Staying At The 5 Star Hotel In The Shard London - SO UPSET!
body: I used to travel in the UK for 3 weeks out of the month and I learned to appreciate the budget chains. I firmly believe that nobody fulfills their mission better than Premier Inn: Very clean, the most comfortable beds, good breakfast and always conveniently located for a reasonable price. Ibis are similar and even cheaper. Once you go up the scale, you start to encounter disappointment. The more you pay the more scope for disappointment.

title: Staying At The 5 Star Hotel In The Shard London - SO UPSET!
body: As someone who has worked the front desk at a Hyatt Resort in San Diego as well as a Holiday Inn Express in a small college town in Northern California, I can honestly tell you that the better rooms for each price point - whether its better for the view, placement in the hotel, or most modern/well furnished rooms without wear - are pretty much only given to those who book direct with the hotel. When an Expedia or other 3rd party booking comes in, its generally assigned to one of the rooms left over after the front desk has made the top room assignments for the reservations that either booked direct or have some sort of standing with that specific hotel/chain; the "left overs" are usually the rooms that are outdated, or close to an elevator, or lacking a view, etc

title: Steve Jobs' 2005 Stanford Commencement Address
body: “Your time is limited, so don’t waste it living someone else’s life. Don’t be trapped by dogma which is living with the results of other people’s thinking. Don’t let the noise of others’ opinions drown out your own inner voice. And the most important, is to have the courage to follow your heart and intuition. They somehow already know what you truly want to become. Everything else is secondary.”

author: @who-hoo-man

title: HARVARD PRE-MED *REALISTIC* DAY-IN-MY-LIFE
body:
As a US med student at a prestigious program, now halfway through my third year, I’ll just share a little unsolicited advice. Make sure to take time for yourself and enjoy your undergraduate experience. Kudos to you for putting in all this work in extracurriculars and resumé building; it’s clear that you’re highly motivated. I will say, though, that a career in medicine is a LONG road and you can only go 100mph for so long before burning out.

The field of medicine is full of neuroticism which applies constant needless pressure to be the best, but I promise you it will be fine and work out well in the end. Take time for yourself: get enough sleep, watch a new series, hang out with friends, visit family. Taking care of yourself in these ways is essential because, otherwise, you may become resentful or regret going into the field.

I also promise that there will be enough time for the essentials (GPA, MCAT prep, research, clinical experience, applications, etc), and you will be more successful in them if you’re healthy in mind and body. Sorry for the rant and I wish the best for you and your future career in our field!
author: @ToastyMuffinz

title: HARVARD PRE-MED *REALISTIC* DAY-IN-MY-LIFE
body: All hardworking people students out there, please don’t forget to rest. Even robots malfunction if they do too much. Protect yourself. Hydrate and sleep.

title: HARVARD PRE-MED *REALISTIC* DAY-IN-MY-LIFE

title: HARVARD PRE-MED *REALISTIC* DAY-IN-MY-LIFE

author: @milo__thefish

title: HARVARD PRE-MED *REALISTIC* DAY-IN-MY-LIFE
body:
It may be easier said than done, but please prioritize your overall health (physical, mental, and emotional). If you are not careful, the load of college and trying to be on top of things chains you. I remember during undergrad and graduate school, I developed gastritis because I was not eating well. I would only get 3 hours of sleep per night. My mental health sank. I had panic attacks to the point I thought I was experiencing a heart attack. I went to the clinic, and they had to send me to the hospital.

Something someone told me, “Do you think your college degree is going to help you when you are a patient in the hospital or six feet under? No. Try creating a balance”.

Please try taking care of yourself now, or else spend the remainder trying to fix it.
author: @donelmalahoo6683

title: HARVARD PRE-MED *REALISTIC* DAY-IN-MY-LIFE
body: This reminds me of my junior/senior years in high school. It was just go go go no breaks gotta keep up with homework, Extracurriculars, work, internship, personal projects, etc. It was absolutely draining. I’m halfway through the first year of college and I no longer stress myself out like that. It’s not worth it. I can still achieve success leading a slower life
author: @audreyguo13

title: HARVARD PRE-MED *REALISTIC* DAY-IN-MY-LIFE
body: I am a final year med student who used to work just like that. The chronic stress gave me a brutal adrenal insufficiency diagnosis. So watch out to your sleep and overall health
author: @SaraKho

title: HARVARD PRE-MED *REALISTIC* DAY-IN-MY-LIFE
body: I‘m a German med studeng and I’m so happy to not have to live like this. I work hard as well, but I keep an eye on sleeping enough and I always keep my sundays study free. Wouldn’t want to risk my health and well being for a prestigious uni😢
author: @elske7736

title: HARVARD PRE-MED *REALISTIC* DAY-IN-MY-LIFE
body: i worked 3 jobs my first two years of a biomedical engineering undergrad then decided to pick up biochem during my second year even tho i had never done chem before. i was so tired i was seeing spots, couldn’t concentrate and once passed out on a train. best thing i did was cut back and focus on only 1-2 jobs. then take summer and winter subjects so i could perform well in uni without burning out. u don’t have to give everything up but SPACE IT OUT. because you can (and probs will) get physically sick and that WILL push you behind. the people i’ve met who perform the best are smart with their study. if you need to sleep then sleep, because when you’re that tired you won’t remember it anyway and it’s useless work. u might be fine now… but last year undergrad is when that burnout will start to hit HARD.
author: @Zoe-no1sg

title: HARVARD PRE-MED *REALISTIC* DAY-IN-MY-LIFE
body: Pre Med is BRUTAL. nobody talks about the stress this causes. constantly trying to keep a 3.7+ score a 515+ on the mcat, hundreds of hours of volunteering, research, and keeping friends.
author: @jordanrigsby9946

title: HARVARD PRE-MED *REALISTIC* DAY-IN-MY-LIFE

title: HARVARD PRE-MED *REALISTIC* DAY-IN-MY-LIFE

title: HARVARD PRE-MED *REALISTIC* DAY-IN-MY-LIFE
body: And that`s how I got depression and anxiety. I study Applied Math and Comuter Science at MIPT but my stupid struggle hustle made me hate it. I am slowly regaining enjoyment from studying but it makes me sad because it feels like I used to know how to work hard play hard at school but fell for this hustle be productive mentality in uni and it was a huge mistake.
author: @MaySpencer

title: HARVARD PRE-MED *REALISTIC* DAY-IN-MY-LIFE
body: I wanted to say thank you for what you do. I’ve been hospitalized 7 times since I was 25. And I only met one nurse who was rude and had an attitude about everything and she was like this with everyone. But hospitalized 7 times and some of those times for over a week, that’s a lot of nurses, and you guys all remain so calm, and are the sweetest people on earth, idk how you guys do it, even with patients giving you a bad time. I’ve never said the words thank you so much in my life, but in the hospital. I love you all and thank you guys for taking care of us sick people, who need you there for us this goes to the doctors also.
author: @RogerCollectz

title: COLLEGE DECISIONS REACTIONS VLOG + announcing where i'm going to college | UCs and Cal States
body: Ucla is a very hard school to get into, it’s acceptance rate is literally a single digit. Just because Nicole is a popular youtuber, and her viewers, including myself, can see how hard she worked to try and guarantee a spot, that doesn’t automatically mean that she should’ve gotten it. Thousands of applicants apply with the same stats as Nicole, some higher and some lower, so just because we can see that she’s studied hard for good grades, and she’s participated in many events that the school has hosted, who are we to assume that all of the other applicants haven’t done the exact same? God has a plan for Nicole, and that’s why she was accepted by sdsu and decided to go there instead. Do you think leaving hate comments on ucla’s Instagram and other social networks will benefit Nicole or you?

title: COLLEGE DECISIONS REACTIONS VLOG + announcing where i'm going to college | UCs and Cal States
body: rejection is actually so hard and it hurts so bad. I am such a believer in that rejection is redirection. You put everything into your application and even though you did not get in its completely okay. Youre going to end up exactly where you are supposed to and go through everything that you need to in order to be successful. I'm from Canada and I have many friends who didnt get into a lot of programs they hoped to and they now realize why they got rejected in terms of it being redirection. A lot of my friends who got rejected from programs they thought were perfect allowed for them to go through completely different experiences and see what they love and what they want to pursue. A perspective they would have never had if they went to the program that they wanted so much  :))

title: COLLEGE DECISIONS REACTIONS VLOG + announcing where i'm going to college | UCs and Cal States
body: Just for some support, my daughter was rejected by her top choice (USC), cried, got accepted to some UC schools (like Davis), and accepted by SDSU. She chose San Diego State, and had an unbelievable four years (Honors College, a cappella group that made it to nationals, sorority, friends forever). She is now on a full scholarship in law school in D.C., and already has worked three law internships/externships over her first two years, with a fourth one this fall. Things will be great - work hard (as you clearly do), know your professors, don’t miss classes, and be engaged in campus activities you like.  Go Aztecs ❤️🖤

title: If Cornell was honest #college #collegeadmissions #ivyleague #cornell
body: Just moved to Ithaca as an adult with a real job.  Ithaca is fun but having a car definitely makes things A LOT more accessible.  I get why some of the undergrads feel trapped up on the hill.  There isn't even a train station, so getting out of town can be hard.  But it is a wonderful place to live!
author: @hilarykristina

title: If Cornell was honest #college #collegeadmissions #ivyleague #cornell
body: I went to Cornell and made a ton of important connections and when I applied to grad school (medical school) a ton of my interviewers were Cornell alum and we were able to connect and just hit off immediately due to the connection.  I eventually went to NYU, where a solid 10% of my classmates were Cornell alum and had a fantastic time.  I’m now starting my academic career in medicine on the west coast, where, again I run into and hit it off with a gaggle of former Cornell grads and have instant connection.  Cornell changed my life, when I can, I will donate back to my Alma mater
author: @boy531

title: If Cornell was honest #college #collegeadmissions #ivyleague #cornell
body: I spent a total of 16 years in Ithaca. 4 yrs undergrad, 8 yrs work, then 4 yrs at the vet school. It's a fine place with natural beauty. I never got bored or lacked for something fun to do. I don't know what this man's problem is.
author: @Torgo1969

title: If Cornell was honest #college #collegeadmissions #ivyleague #cornell
body: I attend Cornell, and there are countless aspects of my school that I genuinely value, including numerous activities like sports, games, club events and so on. I never feel "stuck" inside the walls of Cornell - In fact, I only wish I could have so much time to explore everything that my school has to offer. I never experience any form of inferiority complex because of my association with this institution. You mentioned Andy Bernard and I must confess that I didn't know who he was until you brought him up, so any reference to him is quite irrelevant. It's essential to recognize that allowing one individual to shape your perception of an entire group is a flawed approach. Not everyone gets admitted into a competitive school because of a good SAT score. Instances of legacy admissions are present in various prestigious institutions, including Harvard and Yale, and drawing broad conclusions based on such factors is, in essence, an immature perspective. Let's focus on appreciating the unique qualities of each academic institution without letting isolated incidents overshadow the collective experiences of its diverse student body.
author: @moreblessingmushohwe6506

body: What has frustrated me is people (both Stanford students and others) telling me I should follow my fiancé to Stanford even though I have a great position somewhere else. Some don't even bother to ask what I do first.

title: What's It Like Inside the BEST Public University In the World? | UC Berkeley Campus Tour
body:
What a nice video. I spent the summer'15 in Berkeley for a internship in JBEI, in Emeryville, and my first week was exactly at this frat that u shown. But as i had to wake up very early for job, and there was a lot of people crazy for fun in the frat, it was too much for me, and I moved to I-House, but every weekend i was in the fraternity hahahaha.
I miss this place so much. Berkeley is in my heart ❤
author: @vivianijardim2599
